questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy presents with a 2-day history of low-grade fever, colicky abdominal pain, and palpable purpura over his buttocks and lower legs. His mother notes he had a sore throat one week ago. Urine dipstick shows 1+ blood and 1+ protein. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The triad of palpable purpura (non-thrombocytopenic), abdominal pain, and arthritis/arthralgia following an upper respiratory tract infection is classic for IgA vasculitis. Urine dipstick abnormalities suggest renal involvement. Meningococcal sepsis is fulminant with haemodynamic instability; ITP produces petechiae not palpable purpura; appendicitis localises to right lower quadrant; SLE is unusual at this age and typically not post-infectious.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man presents with a 2-week history of palpable purpura on his legs, mild arthralgia of both ankles, and intermittent abdominal pain. He has no history of liver disease or cryoglobulinaemia. Urine dipstick shows no blood or protein. Skin biopsy shows leukocytoclastic vasculitis with IgA deposition on immunofluorescence. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: IgA deposition on skin biopsy is pathognomonic for IgA vasculitis. Hypersensitivity vasculitis is a clinical diagnosis without IgA deposition; cryoglobulinaemic vasculitis requires cryoglobulins; ANCA vasculitis typically involves upper/lower respiratory tracts and kidneys; polyarteritis nodosa is systemic with mononeuritis and lacks IgA deposition.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with acute-onset palpable purpura on her lower limbs and buttocks, without abdominal pain or arthritis. She has no fever or systemic symptoms. Full blood count, coagulation screen, and renal function are normal. Urine dipstick is negative. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Palpable purpura in a young adult with normal platelets can represent an atypical presentation of IgA vasculitis limited to the skin. ITP causes non-palpable petechiae; meningococcal septicaemia is septic; drug eruption is typically non-palpable and pruritic; acute haemorrhagic oedema occurs in infants and is dramatic.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old girl presents with severe colicky abdominal pain, vomiting, and red-currant jelly stools. On examination, there is a palpable mass in the right upper quadrant and diffuse palpable purpura over her legs and buttocks. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Intussusception is a known complication of IgA vasculitis due to submucosal haemorrhage acting as a lead point. The triad of right upper quadrant mass, red-currant jelly stools, and classic purpura points to intussusception secondary to IgA vasculitis. Meckel's and appendicitis are not associated with purpura; necrotising enterocolitis is a neonatal condition.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with no prior medical history presents with a 1-week history of palpable purpura over his lower legs and arthralgia of both knees. He has not taken any new medications. Serum creatinine is 180 µmol/L (baseline 80 µmol/L), and urine dipstick shows 2+ blood and 1+ protein. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: IgA vasculitis can occur in adults, often with prominent renal involvement. The absence of drug history, negative ANCA, and typical purpura favour IgA vasculitis. ANCA vasculitis usually lacks purpura; cryoglobulinaemia requires hepatitis C; endocarditis has cardiac signs; drug-induced is ruled out by history.

questions.stem: A 7-year-old boy presents with a palpable purpuric rash over his lower legs and buttocks, abdominal pain, and arthralgia of the knees and ankles. He had an upper respiratory tract infection 1 week ago. His urine dipstick is negative. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Skin punch biopsy with direct immunofluorescence
Urine dipstick for blood and protein
questions.explanation: Skin biopsy with direct immunofluorescence showing IgA deposition in small vessel walls is the gold standard for diagnosing IgA vasculitis. Urine dipstick assesses renal involvement but does not confirm the diagnosis. Blood culture and throat swab may identify triggers but are not diagnostic. ANCA is typically negative in IgA vasculitis and helps differentiate from other vasculitides.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old girl with a 2-day history of palpable purpura on her lower limbs and mild abdominal pain is found to have 2+ blood and 1+ protein on urine dipstick. Her blood pressure is normal. What is the most appropriate next investigation to assess for renal involvement?
options: Serum creatinine and estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR)
questions.explanation: Serum creatinine with eGFR is the recommended baseline renal function test in children with abnormal urinalysis. 24-hour urine protein quantifies proteinuria but is not the immediate next test. Renal biopsy is reserved for persistent nephrotic-range proteinuria or worsening renal function. Renal ultrasound may exclude structural anomalies but does not assess function. Complement levels are normal in IgA vasculitis and help distinguish from lupus nephritis.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man presents with a 3-day history of palpable purpura over his lower legs and ankles, and bilateral ankle arthritis. He has no abdominal pain, and his urine dipstick is normal. Which single investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis of IgA vasculitis?
options: Throat swab for group A streptococcus
questions.explanation: Skin biopsy with direct immunofluorescence showing IgA deposition is diagnostic even in the absence of gastrointestinal or renal involvement. ANCA, cryoglobulins, and rheumatoid factor are useful to exclude other vasculitides but are not diagnostic for IgA vasculitis. Throat swab may identify a trigger but does not confirm the disease.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old boy presents with widespread palpable purpura, colicky abdominal pain, and a swollen right knee. His urine dipstick is negative. He is afebrile. A surgical opinion excludes intussusception. What is the most appropriate investigation to differentiate IgA vasculitis from other causes of small vessel vasculitis?
questions.explanation: Skin biopsy with immunofluorescence demonstrating IgA deposition is specific for IgA vasculitis and distinguishes it from ANCA-associated vasculitides, cryoglobulinemic vasculitis, and lupus vasculitis. ANCA serology helps identify granulomatosis with polyangiitis or microscopic polyangiitis. CT abdomen is used to assess for intussusception, not for diagnosis. Platelet count is normal in IgA vasculitis and elevated in thrombotic microangiopathies. D-dimer is nonspecific.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with a known history of systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) presents with palpable purpura over her lower legs, arthralgia, and colicky abdominal pain. Urine dipstick shows protein 2+. Which of the following findings would best support a diagnosis of IgA vasculitis rather than a lupus vasculitis?
options: Serum C3 and C4 levels within normal range
Skin biopsy showing IgA deposition in dermal small vessels
questions.explanation: Direct immunofluorescence of skin showing granular IgA deposits is pathognomonic for IgA vasculitis. In lupus vasculitis, immune complexes contain IgG, IgM, and C3, not predominantly IgA. Normal complement levels and negative anti-dsDNA are not specific for IgA vasculitis, as they can occur in SLE remission. IgA rheumatoid factor can be present in both conditions but is not discriminatory. Low cryoglobulins are common in essential mixed cryoglobulinemia, not typically in IgA vasculitis.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old man presents with a 3-day history of palpable purpura over his lower legs, arthralgia of both ankles, and mild abdominal discomfort. There is no haematuria or proteinuria. Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment for his joint pain?
questions.explanation: In mild IgA vasculitis without renal or severe GI involvement, simple analgesia with paracetamol is first-line. NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen) are relatively contraindicated due to risk of worsening renal function or GI bleeding. Steroids are reserved for severe disease. Colchicine and dapsone are not indicated.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with known IgA vasculitis presents with severe colicky abdominal pain, haematochezia, and vomiting. CT abdomen shows bowel wall thickening but no intussusception. Which is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g daily for 3 days
questions.explanation: For severe GI involvement in IgA vasculitis, high-dose corticosteroids (e.g., IV methylprednisolone) are first-line to reduce vasculitic inflammation. IVIG is used in Kawasaki disease, not HSP. Cyclophosphamide is reserved for severe renal disease. Omeprazole treats symptoms only. Surgery is considered only if perforation or intussusception occurs.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with IgA vasculitis has nephritic syndrome: haematuria, proteinuria 2.5 g/day, and serum creatinine rising to 200 µmol/L. Renal biopsy shows mesangial IgA deposition with crescents in 30% of glomeruli. Which is the most appropriate first-line disease-modifying therapy?
options: Rituximab 1 g intravenously on days 1 and 15
questions.explanation: In proliferative IgA nephropathy with nephrotic-range proteinuria and active crescents, oral corticosteroids (e.g., prednisolone) are first-line immunosuppression. ACE inhibitors (ramipril) are supportive for proteinuria but not anti-inflammatory. Mycophenolate, cyclophosphamide, and rituximab are reserved for severe or steroid-resistant disease.

questions.stem: An 8-year-old boy presents with classic palpable purpura, arthralgia of both knees, and mild colicky abdominal pain. He has no haematuria or proteinuria. Which drug is contraindicated for symptom relief?
questions.explanation: NSAIDs (e.g., ibuprofen) are contraindicated in suspected IgA vasculitis because they can exacerbate renal dysfunction (even if initially normal) and increase GI bleeding risk. Paracetamol is a safe analgesic. Codeine is less preferred but not contraindicated. Steroids are not indicated for mild disease. Omeprazole is for GI symptoms but not contraindicated.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman in her second trimester of pregnancy presents with a flare of IgA vasculitis: new palpable purpura on her legs, bilateral ankle arthritis, and mild abdominal pain. Urinalysis shows no protein or blood. Which is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Ibuprofen 400 mg three times daily as needed
Intravenous immunoglobulin 1 g/kg daily for 2 days
Prednisolone 40 mg once daily for 5 days then taper
questions.explanation: Corticosteroids (prednisolone) are the first-line therapy for moderate-to-severe flares in IgA vasculitis and are safe in pregnancy. NSAIDs should be avoided in the second and third trimesters. Methotrexate is teratogenic and contraindicated. IVIG is not first-line for HSP; dapsone has no role in acute treatment.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy presents with a two-day history of a palpable purpuric rash on his lower limbs, bilateral ankle swelling, and colicky abdominal pain. His temperature is 37.1°C, pulse 110 bpm, BP 110/70 mmHg. Urine dipstick shows 2+ protein and 1+ blood. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Check serial blood pressures and urine dipstick
Prescribe oral ibuprofen for joint pain
questions.explanation: In IgA vasculitis, renal involvement is the key determinant of long-term outcome. Immediate monitoring of blood pressure and urinalysis is essential to detect progression. Steroids are not indicated for mild rash/arthralgia; ultrasound is reserved for suspected intussusception; NSAIDs are avoided with renal impairment; biopsy is not immediate.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old female presents with a three-day history of palpable purpura on her legs and mild arthralgia in her knees. She has no abdominal pain. Urine dipstick is negative for protein and blood. BP 120/75 mmHg. CRP is mildly elevated. What is the most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Reassure and advise to monitor urine dipstick weekly
Refer to dermatology for skin biopsy
questions.explanation: This is mild disease with no renal or GI involvement. Supportive care with reassurance and monitoring of renal status is appropriate. NSAIDs can be used but with caution; steroids are reserved for severe arthralgia or organ-threatening disease; skin biopsy is unnecessary; methotrexate is for chronic refractory cases.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old male with recently diagnosed IgA vasculitis presents to the ED with severe colicky abdominal pain, vomiting, and one episode of haematochezia. His abdomen is mildly distended with generalised tenderness. BP 110/60 mmHg, pulse 110 bpm. What is the most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Insert a nasogastric tube and commence bowel rest
Request a surgical review for possible intussusception
questions.explanation: Intussusception is a known surgical complication of IgA vasculitis, presenting with colicky pain, vomiting, and haematochezia. Urgent surgical review is the priority to confirm and manage this. Steroids may be indicated later; endoscopy is not diagnostic; IVIG is not standard; NG tube is supportive but not most immediate.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with IgA vasculitis diagnosed two weeks ago presents with periorbital and lower limb oedema, hypertension (BP 160/100 mmHg), and urine dipstick showing 3+ protein and 2+ blood. Serum creatinine is 180 µmol/L (baseline 80). What is the most appropriate immediate next action?
questions.explanation: Moderate-to-severe renal involvement requires specialist nephrology input for further management, including biopsy and immunosuppression. The immediate step is referral. ACE inhibitors may be started but need close monitoring; biopsy is not urgent before referral; steroids and cyclophosphamide are decisions for nephrologist.

questions.stem: A 5-year-old boy with IgA vasculitis presents with acute severe left testicular pain and swelling. He has a purpuric rash on his legs. He is afebrile. What is the most appropriate immediate next action?
options: Apply a scrotal support and reassess
questions.explanation: Testicular involvement in IgA vasculitis can mimic torsion. Urgent ultrasound is required to distinguish between inflammatory swelling and torsion. Antibiotics are for infection; NSAIDs for pain but after diagnosis; surgical exploration is indicated if torsion confirmed; scrotal support is not immediate for acute pain.

questions.stem: A 6-year-old boy presents with palpable purpura on his buttocks and legs, arthralgia of both knees, and mild abdominal pain. He has vomited twice. Which single clinical finding would most urgently suggest the development of intussusception?
questions.explanation: Bilious vomiting is a classic sign of small bowel obstruction, which in the context of Henoch-Schönlein purpura indicates intussusception until proven otherwise. Non-bilious vomiting, abdominal distension, and palpable mass can occur in other abdominal conditions, but bilious vomiting is the most specific red flag for mechanical obstruction requiring urgent surgical review.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman with Henoch-Schönlein purpura presents with palpable purpura, arthralgia, and mild colicky abdominal pain. Urine dipstick shows 2+ blood and 1+ protein. Which single finding would indicate the need for urgent renal consultation?
questions.explanation: Oliguria is an acute red flag for rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis and acute kidney injury, requiring immediate nephrology input. While raised creatinine and hypertension are concerning, oliguria represents a more acute severity that demands urgent assessment. Proteinuria and oedema are common but not emergent alone.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with known Henoch-Schönlein purpura on corticosteroid therapy suddenly develops severe dyspnoea and hypoxia. Oxygen saturations are 88% on room air. Which single clinical finding would most strongly suggest pulmonary haemorrhage as the cause?
options: Decreased breath sounds at the right base
Fine crackles at the lung bases
questions.explanation: Haemoptysis is the hallmark of pulmonary haemorrhage, a rare but life-threatening complication of Henoch-Schönlein purpura from pulmonary capillaritis. Crackles and wheeze are non-specific, and a pleural rub or decreased breath sounds suggest alternative diagnoses. Haemoptysis is the definitive red flag for this emergency.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with Henoch-Schönlein purpura on low-dose prednisolone presents with acute onset of severe left testicular pain and swelling. On examination, the left testis is tender and swollen. Which single physical finding best distinguishes testicular torsion from vasculitic involvement?
options: Firm, tender testis in a horizontal lie
Absent cremasteric reflex on the ipsilateral side
Palpable nodule on the testicular surface
questions.explanation: Absent cremasteric reflex is a highly sensitive sign for testicular torsion and should raise immediate suspicion. A horizontal lie is also suggestive but often less reliable in acute assessment. Prehn sign is typically positive in epididymitis, not torsion. Scrotal erythema and warmth are non-specific. The absent cremasteric reflex is the key red flag for surgical urgency.

questions.stem: A 75-year-old man presents with new-onset palpable purpura on his lower legs, arthralgia, and a serum creatinine of 150 µmol/L. He has no history of recent infection. Which single clinical finding should raise suspicion for ANCA-associated vasculitis rather than Henoch-Schönlein purpura?
options: Mononeuritis multiplex of the lower limbs
questions.explanation: Mononeuritis multiplex is a classic feature of microscopic polyangiitis (an ANCA-associated vasculitis) and is very rare in Henoch-Schönlein purpura. Epistaxis and nasal crusting are more typical of granulomatosis with polyangiitis. Crescentic glomerulonephritis can occur in both, but the clinical finding of mononeuritis multiplex is the key discriminating red flag for ANCA disease.
